summary: The author secured 3rd place by combining synthetic velocity data generation with a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture. Key technical choices included modifying the OpenFWI forward model for numerical stability, using random velocity blending to augment training data, and implementing a multi-stage training pipeline with progressively larger input resolutions. A major driver of performance was switching from AdamW to the Muon optimizer, which significantly reduced validation error. Final predictions were generated by blending models from three training stages and applying strict value clipping.
extensive_summary:
## Forward Modeling & Data Generation
The pipeline begins with synthetic data generation to overcome data limitations. The author adapted an existing OpenFWI implementation, changing the dtype to float32, expanding inputs to 6 channels for fast horizontal flip augmentation, and porting it to PyTorch for GPU batch execution. To address numerical discrepancies in the forward model, the last row of seismic data was zeroed during training. Training data was augmented via random blending of two velocities (alpha in [0.3, 0.7]) from the same fold, both restricted to the same velocity type and unrestricted across types, repeated across 8 seeds.

## Modeling Architecture
The core model is a Vision Transformer with RoPE, initially prototyped with `eva02_base_patch16_clip_224` and finalized as `eva02_large_patch14_clip_224`. Inputs are rearranged from `(N, 5, 1000, 70)` to `(N, 5, 250, 280)`, interpolated to `(N, 5, patch_size * 18, patch_size * 18)`, and passed through the ViT. The architecture splits the transformer layers: the first third handles intra-channel feature interaction, channels are average-pooled to one, and the final two-thirds perform further feature interaction. Outputs are pixel-shuffled, passed through a sigmoid, and scaled by 6000.

## Optimization & Training Strategy
Training follows a three-stage progressive resolution approach. Each stage uses horizontal flip augmentation, a mix of constant and cosine decay learning rates, and resumes from the previous stage's weights. The most impactful optimization choice was replacing AdamW with `MuonWithAuxAdam` for all `Linear` layers (except patch embedding), while keeping AdamW for other parameters. This yielded a substantial drop in validation MAE.

## Submission & Post-Processing
The final submission blends models from the three stages in a 20%+30%+50% ratio. Predictions are post-processed with `x.clip(1500, 4500).round()`. The author notes an inference bug regarding the last row zeroing that was corrected before final submission.
competition_flow: Raw seismic/velocity data is processed through a modified OpenFWI forward model and augmented via synthetic velocity blending, fed into a multi-stage Vision Transformer trained with the Muon optimizer, then blended and clipped for the final submission.
data_processing: Expansion to 6 channels for fast hflip
Zeroing the last row of seismic data
ensembling: Models from three training stages were blended in a 20%+30%+50% ratio, followed by value clipping via `x.clip(1500, 4500).round()`.
insights: Generating more synthetic data consistently leads to performance improvements.
The Muon optimizer provides significant gains over AdamW for this task, though it introduces non-negligible overhead with small batch sizes.
Multi-stage training with progressively larger input resolutions effectively improves model performance.
critical_findings: Switching from AdamW to MuonWithAuxAdam dropped validation MAE from 9.x to 7.x, marking the biggest performance takeaway.
OpenFWI forward modeling exhibits large errors in the last row of seismic data for certain velocity types, necessitating row zeroing during training.

other_solution_refs.description: Original forward modeling implementation adapted by the author
